HAPPEN study analysis v1.4

NW, BM

2026-02-19

1 Version details

Current version includes final data check for manuscript preparation.

This report summarises primary, secondary and tertiary outcomes from the HAPPEN trial. Analyses follow details outlined in the Statistical Analysis Plan (https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.08.14.25333719).

Relevant descriptions from the SAP are provided in subsequent sections.

2 Study design

The HAPPEN study design was a stepped-wedge cluster randomised trial across three public hospitals in Australia. The study design was defined by nine clusters, where a single ward was defined as a cluster. Three wards from each participating hospital were enrolled in the study. Wards within each hospital were randomised to one of three treatment sequences, which determined their transition from the control condition (current standard of oral care) to the intervention condition (oral care bundle). No transition phase was assumed between control and intervention conditions.

The planned study design specified all clusters starting in the control condition for the first three months. A single ward from each hospital was then scheduled to switch from the control to intervention condition every 2 months, resuliting in a total trial length of 12 months.

Figure 1: Observed treatment sequences

Observed treatment sequences match the planned design for Hospitals 1 and 2, starting in June 2024. Trial commencement was delayed in Hospital 3 was delayed by two months. The observed treatment sequences at Hospital 3 were therefore shifted to start in August 2024. All hospitals collected data for 12 months. The observed treatment sequences by cluster are in Figure 1.

3 Inclusion/exclusion criteria

Data were provided for 12,474 REDCap records. Initial data cleaning assessed records based on inclusion/exclusion criteria for study enrollment and timing of infections relative to study ward admission and discharge dates.

Additional record exclusions were based on the availability of a patient’s medical records at the time surveillance, missing ward admission and discharges dates need to define patient time at-risk, and time spent on a study ward. A summary of numbers excluded is provided below.

  • Under 18 years of age: 12
  • No medical record available at the time of surveillance (Other reasons): 29
  • No hospital and ward identifiers: 15
  • Duplicate records: 1
  • Missing ward admission and/or discharge dates: 23
  • Admitted to a study ward for less than 48 hours: 3336
  • Discharged from a study ward before the start of the cluster control period: 188

For remaining records, the following infection numbers were excluded based on SAP attribution rules (SAP, p8 - 9).

  • Infection was acquired before study ward admission: 3.
  • Infection acquired on day or study ward admission of within the first 48 hours of admission: 41.
  • Infection acquired within 48 hours of control start: .
  • Infection acquired more than 48 hours after a cluster’s intervention end date: 0.
  • VAP: 8; based on date of HAP onset relative to recorded ventilator start and end dates.

The final sample size for analysis was 8,870.

4 Descriptive statistics

Cohort characteristics were summarised using descriptive statistics by study hospital (Hospital 1, Hospital 2, Hospital 2), study condition at the time of ward admission (Control, Intervention), primary outcome assessment (no HAP, HAP), and overall infection outcome based on assessment of HAP, lower-upper respiratory infections (LURI) and EENT-Oral (No infection, 1 or more infections).

For each cohort stratification in Tables 1 to 5, categorical variables are reported as total events/total records with corresponding percentages. Continuous variables are reported as quantiles, Median (Q1 to Q3), and means with standard deviations, Mean (SD). Total sample size per group are reported in each table.

5 Time at-risk

For analysis, the length of each time step was 1 month. Under this design, it was likely that a patient’s admission would include more than one study month. This feature of the data is explored in Table @ref{tab:admission-months}, which shows the total number of records based on the number of study months included in an admission. Based on the design, each study month started on the first Monday of a calendar month. Inclusion in a study month was defined as 1 day or more.

Table 6: Summary of admissions in terms of total study months spent on a study ward
Number of study months on study ward n % Total records
1 6,158 69.4
2 2,573 29.0
3 112 1.3
4 19 0.2
5 4 0.0
6 2 0.0
8 1 0.0
10 1 0.0

The next table shows similar numbers but by 6-weekly time steps, which define when different clusters transition from control to intervention.

Table 7: Summary of admissions in terms of total study periods spent on a study ward
Number of study periods on study ward (1 study period = 3 months) n % Total records
1 8,003 90.2
2 864 9.7
3 1 0.0
4 2 0.0

Figure 2: Total records analysed by cluster and time step

  • Figure 4 shows percentages by study month (A) and months relative to the start of intervention exposure (B) by hospital.

At the patient level, Figures 7 to 9 show unadjusted cumulative incidence functions for each infection type by study condition. For each figure, cumulative incidence functions are displayed for all observed lengths of ward stay (left panel), and lengths of stay up to 30 days (right panel).

Figure 8: Cumulative incidence of lower/upper respiratory infection by study condition

Figure 9: Cumulative incidence of EENT-Oral infection by study condition

Figure 10: Cumulative proportion of HAP/LURI/EENT-Oral - time to first infection

7 Primary outcome analysis

The primary outcome is non-ventilator-associated hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP). The primary outcome definition is the European Centre for Disease Control definition of HAP.

The primary outcome was analysed at the patient-level and the cluster level. Patient-level analysis modelled the incidence of HAP adjusted for ward days at-risk. The final ward day at-risk was defined as the date of infection onset (HAP) or the date of ward discharge (no HAP).

Cluster-level analysis modelling the number of new HAP events per 100 at-risk admissions, defined as total admissions and admission days at-risk. Model estimates are reported as incidence risk ratios with 95% confidence intervals and p-values.

The main analysis included all study wards. Sensitivity analysis applied a leave-one-cluster-out analysis to assess the sensitivity of estimated intervention effectiveness.

7.1 Patient-level analysis

Table 11 summarises main and sensitivity analysis results for HAP risk at the patient-level. Model estimates are reported as hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals and p-values.

Results are presented as hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals for the main analysis where all clusters are included, and for leave-one-cluster-out sensitivity analysis.

Quantities ending in _c correspond to results following post-correction for the number of clusters. (Degree of freedom correction, t distribution with total included clusters - 1 d.f.)

Table 11: Estimated intervention effectiveness on the incidence of HAP; patient-level analysis
Cluster excluded Hazard ratio 95% CI p.value 95% CI_c p.value_c
Main analysis
None 0.396 0.192 - 0.817 0.012 0.165 - 0.949 0.041
Sensitivity analysis
None - random intervention effect per hospital 0.396 0.192 - 0.818 0.012 0.165 - 0.95 0.041
1 0.355 0.145 - 0.868 0.023 0.116 - 1.08 0.064
2 0.340 0.172 - 0.672 0.002 0.146 - 0.796 0.021
3 0.405 0.192 - 0.854 0.018 0.16 - 1.03 0.055
4 0.487 0.228 - 1.04 0.063 0.189 - 1.25 0.112
5 0.349 0.157 - 0.776 0.010 0.128 - 0.946 0.042
6 0.368 0.167 - 0.807 0.013 0.138 - 0.982 0.047
7 0.380 0.17 - 0.848 0.018 0.139 - 1.03 0.056
8 0.430 0.195 - 0.948 0.036 0.16 - 1.15 0.081
9 0.436 0.199 - 0.954 0.038 0.164 - 1.16 0.083

7.2 Cluster-level analysis

Table 12 shows results of modelling the total number HAPs acquired per 100 ward admissions. For this analysis, patients are included in monthly denominators if they spent at least 1 day on the ward for a given month. Therefore, this analysis does not account for time spent on the ward.

Table 12: Estimated intervention effectiveness on the incidence of HAP; cluster-level analysis per 100 admissions
Cluster excluded Incidence rate ratio 95% CI p.value 95% CI_c p.value_c
Main analysis
None 0.407 0.197 - 0.841 0.015 0.17 - 0.976 0.046
Sensitivity analysis

8 Secondary outcome analysis

Secondary outcomes were respiratory tract (lower and upper combined) and EENT-O infections. Analysis followed the same process as per the primary outcome.

8.1 Lower and upper respiratory infections

9 Tertiary outcome analysis

For total infections, patient-level and cluster-level analysis results are shown in Table 21.

Table 21: Estimated intervention effectiveness for one or more infections during trial ward admission
Analysis OR (patient-level)/IRR (cluster-level) 95% CI p.value 95% CI_c p.value_c

Figure 11 summarises the monthly audit results on oral care, as a measure of intervention fidelity.

Figure 11: Monthly distribution of oral care on the last day of a patients ward admission

Tables 22 and 23 show descriptive statistics for oral care by control and intervention.

Study period n %
Control 473 16
Intervention 1727 62

Table 24 summarises the percentage of patients with documented oral care on the last day of their admission during control and intervention conditions, after adjusting for clustering. Among patients with documented oral care, the average times performed is also reported. Results are presented as point estimates with 95% confidence intervals.

Table 24: Results of oral care
Outcome measure Control Intervention
Oral care performed, % 8; 5.3 to 12 42; 32 to 52
